titleInfo.title: Chengdu diqu zai nanbeichao fojiaoshi shang de zhongyao diwei
成都地區在南北朝佛教史上的重要地位
The Significance of the Chengdu Region in the History of Buddhism during the Northern and Southern Dynasties

mods.abstract: The important discoveries of Buddhist artefacts (Wanfosi 万佛寺 since the Guangxu era of the Qing dynasty 1875-1908, more recently excavations in Shangyejie 商业街 and Xi’an lu 西安路) have already given testimony of Chengdu’s important role during the Northern and Southern dynasties. Characteristics of Buddhist sculpture of the Southern Dynasties in Chengdu: Lei distinguishes three phases, 1. from 424-497, 2a from 497-519, 2b from 520-546, and 3. from 547 to the northern Zhou dynasty, and she names representative works. Phase 1: Screened images 背屏式 and image stele 造像碑, Maitrya, Amitayus, Guanyin, mostly sitting images, Guanyin and Maitreya wearing bodhisattva dress and taking a Buddha’s position, groups of one Buddha and two bodhisattvas, simple dress, Buddha with polished usnisa, vigorous and simple stature, simple mandorla, slender figures, slanting shoulders, bodies thin and weak. Phase 2a: Mainly screened images of Amitayus, Maitreya, Guanyin, Śākyamuni with two bodies, Śākyamuni and Prabhūtaratna, mainly groups of one Buddha and two bodhisattvas, with the main image standing or sitting. Phase 2b: Also groups of one Buddha, four bodhisattvas, four disciples and two guardians; two Buddhas, three bodhisattvas, two disciples, and two guardians; some Buddhas with spiral headdress, but still some with polished usnisa, ornaments of mandorla more elaborate with apsaras, treasure pagoda, and narrative scenes. Phase 3: Many Aśoka images with moustache and kasaya covering both shoulders; Śākyamuni with low usnisa and spiral headdress, worked fully in the round; bodhisattva Maitreya adorned with jewellery, curved body. Historical and cultural background: The style of the earliest phase of sculptures in Chengdu (until ca. 460) is not different from the Northern Wei sculpture of the same time in Yungang, Maijishan, Binglingsi, and Dunhuang. This style derived from the northwest, from Gandhara. Motifs like Maitreya bodhisattva with crossed ankles and the seven Buddhas adorning mandorlas, are also comparable to the stone pagodas 石塔 from the northwestern capital Liangzhou 凉州. In phase 1, Buddhism in Chengdu was closer to Buddhism in the northwest than to Buddhism in the middle and lower reaches of the Changjiang. Only under the Liang dynasty, the Buddhist teaching in Jiankang started to heavily influence Buddhism in Sichuan. At the end of the first phase, the influence from Jiankang already shows in the figures of Maitreya as Buddha from the Southern Qi dynasty (479-502), while the figure of Maitreya as bodhisattva with crossed ankles, also unearthed in Chengdu, was flourishing in the north. Jiankang, the center of the Southern Dynasties, developped the 清谈玄学 Qingtan xuanxue and the elegant and slender 飘逸清瘦 style. As soon as Chengdu became part of the Southern Dynasty, it adopted this style. In phase 2a (end of Southern Qi dynasty until Liang Wudi Tianjian 天监 era (502-519), the style 秀骨清像 flourished in Chengdu. In phase 2b (520-549), the influence of Jiankang on Sichuan reached its climax. Monks served as envoys, travelling from one king to the other, like the eighth son of Liang Wudi, Xiao 萧, who was sent to Chengdu with an entourage of monks. From Chengdu, the southern style reached north China, and influenced the art of Binglingsi, Maijishan, and Longmen. At that time, Liang Wudi also received many envoys who came via the sea from 扶南 Funan and Persia 波斯. With them, a new style spread which was influenced by the painter Zhang Sengzhou 张僧繇, who added some flesh to the emaciated figures. The Aśoka images which dominated Phase 3 reached Sichuan from the northwest. Aśoka images from Chengdu are comparable to those from Binglingsi cave 169, but the style of the Chengdu images still shows elements from the Liang dyansty. In the third phase, Chengdu served as pivot for the traffic from Jiankang to the northwestern regions of China (see the biographies of the monks Tanmomiduo 曇摩密多 and Zhimeng 智猛). Historical administrations: After the demise of the Eastern Han dynasty, three main regions might be identified: Chengdu in the southwest, Chang’an and Luoyang in the north, and Jiankang in the lower reaches of the Changjiang. The Eastern Jin united China in 280, and sixteen years later, people from the northwest entered Sichuan. In 302, the kingdom of Chenghan 成汉政权 (302-347, one of the sixteen kingdoms of Jin 265-420) was established in Chengdu. In 347, Chengdu was returned to the Eastern Jin dynasty (at the lower reaches of the Changjiang). The sinicized Eastern Jin had established itself in Jiankang 30 years earlier. The reign of the Chenghan kingdom brought relative stability to the region: the north of China and the central plain was at the same time torn between the five nomadic tribes and three Han generals who established 16 states (304-439). In 373, a nomadic tribe conquered Sichuan and established the Qian Qin dynasty which ruled the region until 383, and then started to collapse, giving way once more to several warlords. In 413, Chengdu was returned one more time to the Esatern Jin dyansty, which itself ended in 420. Afterwards, Chengdu belonged to the Southern Dynasties of Song, Qi, and Liang until 553. In 548, the marquis of Jing 侯景 took control over the central administration of the Liang, and the clan of the Xiao ruled, but still used the Liang dynasties era names. In 551, Xiao Ji 萧纪 declared himself emperor in Chengdu, but the Western Wei dynasty had also gained a hold of Sichuan territory. In 556, the Northern Zhou dynasty finally controlled Chengdu, until 581, when the Sui united the empire. In the 264 years between 317 and 581, Chengdu was part of the Southern dynasties for only 130 years. Its status is therefore very different from the central plains with Chang’an and Luoyang, or from the south with Jiankang 建康 and Xiangyang 襄阳. When war raged in the north of China, Chengdu became the passageway for contacts between the central plain and the western regions. These three regions each had its own culture. Even under the unified empires of the Qin and Han, these three regions preserved cultural diversities, for example regarding burial rites. On the other hand, archaeological findings prove of cultural contacts between the Gansu corridor and Chengdu as early as the neolithic period. From the Warring States period to the Han dynasty, people from Gansu migrated to the region west of Chengdu in large numbers. The unification under the Han dynasty effected some similarities among the three regions. But at the end of the third and the beginning of the fourth century, almost a hundred thousand refugees from the north (nomadic tribes of the Di Qiang 氐羌)came to Sichuan, and finally established the Chenghan rule in Chengdu, driving the Ba and Shu people out of Chengdu . They moved to Jing 荆 and Xiang 湘 in today Hunan. The people of the Qian Qin dynasty who ruled Chengdu at the end of the fourth century had also come from the northwest. Therefore, from the 3rd to the 6th century, the central plain, Chengdu and the lower Changjiang regions had different cultural traditions. Many rulers in the north had close relations to eminent Buddhist monks. For example, Fotucheng 佛图澄 influenced the Latter Zhao rulers Shile 石勒 (305-333) and Shihu 石虎 with auguries. When Shihu transferred the capital to Ye in 334, Buddhist monks started to proselytize in the south. Lei further corroborates the routes of exchange and travel between the western regions, the central plain, Chengdu and the Lower Changjiang region by referring to the biographies of the following monks: Zhimeng 智猛 (-404-425-437-), Tanmomiduo 曇摩密多, 僵良耶舍Kālayaśas, Tanhong 曇弘(ca. 400–455), and Chaobian 超辨 (411-483).
